text: Think of an undirected graph like a two-way street, where you can travel in both directions.

payload.heading_left: What are the key characteristics of undirected graphs in computer science?

payload.text: Undirected graphs have no direction of edges

text: In an undirected graph, edges don't point in any direction, so if there's an edge between two vertices, you can move between them freely.

payload.text: Edges in undirected graphs are bidirectional

text: This is different from directed graphs, where edges have direction and represent a one-way relationship.
Let's look at an example to understand this better.
Consider a graph with nodes A, B, C, and D, where each node is connected to its neighbors without any direction.

payload.text: Nodes in undirected graphs are connected freely

text: As we discuss undirected graphs, notice how the edges connect the nodes, allowing us to move between them in any direction.

payload.text: Undirected graphs have bidirectional edges, allowing for free movement between nodes.
